title: Refuting Your Legislators' Excuses for Not Abolishing Abortion
author: Tim Gillespie
orientation: Tim Gillespie, drawing on his lobbying experience with the Oklahoma Second Amendment Association, walks abolitionists through the Oklahoma legislative process and dismantles the most common excuses lawmakers give for refusing to advance an abolition bill. The piece equips constituents to push back when legislators hide behind procedure, staff, committee assignments, or scheduling.
audiences: Citizen lobbyist visiting the Oklahoma Capitol for the first time
Pastor mobilizing his congregation to contact representatives
State legislator weighing whether to author or co-sponsor an abolition bill
Pro-life voter who keeps getting procedural answers from elected officials
Abolitionist organizer planning a session-long advocacy strategy

questions.question: Why does it matter that the Oklahoma Legislature operates on a two-year cycle when I'm trying to get an abolition bill heard?
questions.audience: Citizen lobbyist visiting the Oklahoma Capitol for the first time
questions.answer: Bills filed in the first year of a legislature don't die at the end of that session. They remain alive for the second year, which means an abolition bill that was bottled up in 2021 is still in play in 2022. Understanding the cycle keeps activists from giving up too early or accepting the claim that a bill is 'dead' before it actually is.
questions.quote: The legislature works on a two-year cycle.  The 58th Legislature was seated following the 2020 elections.  They have two regular sessions: the 2021 Session and the 2022 Session. Bills submitted for the 2021 session that were not either voted down or voted through are still alive for the 2022 Session.

questions.question: When can a bill actually be considered 'dead' for the session?
questions.audience: Abolitionist organizer planning a session-long advocacy strategy
questions.answer: Procedural deadlines are not absolute. Leaders can route around them, so a bill is only truly dead once it has been voted down on the merits or once the legislature gavels out at the end of the second session of the cycle. Organizers should keep pressing through every supposed deadline.
questions.quote: That said, there are ways around these deadlines. No bill is ever really dead until it is either voted down or until the final gavel falls in late May of even-numbered years.

questions.question: What is a 'shell bill' and why should abolitionists know the term?
questions.audience: Citizen lobbyist visiting the Oklahoma Capitol for the first time
questions.answer: A shell bill is a near-empty bill containing only the title of law and the title of the act, with the substantive language added later by amendment or substitute. Knowing this lets activists recognize when a legislator is keeping a vehicle alive that could later carry abolition language, rather than being fooled by the bare bones text.
questions.quote: A term you will hear often is "shell bill." A shell bill is a bill that has minimal language in it, usually just the title of law and the title of the act.

questions.question: If a legislator says 'this bill has to go to the health committee,' how should I respond?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter who keeps getting procedural answers from elected officials
questions.answer: Push back. There is no rule binding an abolition bill to a particular committee. Gillespie points to multiple committees that could legitimately receive an abolition bill, including Judiciary, Public Safety, and the House State Powers Committee, and notes the committee's name is largely irrelevant.
questions.quote: In short, there are any number of committees to which a bill of abolition could be assigned. In fact, the committee name really doesn't matter at all. Do not let them tell you otherwise.

questions.question: Why does Tim Gillespie insist abolition bills belong in Title 21 rather than Title 63?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing whether to author or co-sponsor an abolition bill
questions.answer: Title 63 is the public health and safety code, which is appropriate for regulating abortion as health care. Title 21 is the criminal code where homicide laws live. Because abolitionists treat abortion as murder, the law criminalizing it must be placed alongside other homicide statutes, not among medical regulations.
questions.quote: If your goal is to regulate abortion, then yes, Title 63 is where the law belongs. Our goal is not the regulation of abortion as health care.  The criminalization of abortion belongs with the other homicide laws in Title 21. Do not let them tell you otherwise.

questions.question: When my representative says 'I'll have to get with staff,' is that a legitimate answer?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter who keeps getting procedural answers from elected officials
questions.answer: It can be a stalling or blame-shifting tactic. Staff serve at the pleasure of the legislator, not the reverse. Gillespie has personally watched legislators correct staff errors, so the constituent should make clear that the legislator, not unelected staff, is responsible for the bill.
questions.quote: The respective staffs work for the legislators, not the other way around. However, weak-kneed legislators that want to avoid controversy will often try to deflect blame onto the House or Senate staff.

questions.question: What is 'striking title' and why is it dangerous for an abolition bill?
questions.audience: Abolitionist organizer planning a session-long advocacy strategy
questions.answer: Striking title removes the section-of-law designation that a bill must have to be enacted. The Senate uses the maneuver routinely, sometimes legitimately to keep a bill alive while wording is fixed, but often as a quiet way to kill the bill, because a hostile colleague can simply refuse to consent to reinstating the title.
questions.quote: A bill cannot be passed into law without its Title. Remember, the Title is the section of law where the new law will reside. It is its permanent residential address, so to speak. You cannot put a law into statutes if you don't specify where it goes.

questions.question: How do I tell whether a strike-title is a real working pause or a stealth kill?
questions.audience: Abolitionist organizer planning a session-long advocacy strategy
questions.answer: Watch the posture of the legislator who moved to strike title. If that person is hostile to the bill, they will simply withhold consent to reinstate the title, ending the bill quietly. Friendly intent versus hostile intent is the tell.
questions.quote: It is easy enough to determine which is the case. If the one asking to strike the Title is hostile toward the bill, he simply will not give consent to reinstate the title, thus killing the bill. Do not let them tell you otherwise.

questions.question: Why might a legislator file an abolition bill they have no intention of pushing?
questions.audience: Pastor mobilizing his congregation to contact representatives
questions.answer: Filing the bill placates a constituent who asked for it. Behind the scenes the legislator can quietly tell the committee chair not to hear it, sometimes with caucus-wide coordination. The bill exists on paper while everyone involved deliberately ensures it goes nowhere, deceiving the activists who requested it.
questions.quote: If a constituent approaches their representative or senator with an issue, sometimes they will submit a bill just because they don't want to anger a constituent but will tell the chairman they don't really want them to hear the bill. Sometimes this is done between the representative/senator and the chair and sometimes this is a caucus-wide decision. In other words, there is often collusion with the intent to dupe a constituent.

questions.question: When my legislator blames the committee chair, the pro tem, or the governor, how should I evaluate that?
questions.audience: Pro-life voter who keeps getting procedural answers from elected officials
questions.answer: Treat blame-shifting as evidence of complicity. A legislator who refuses to use the influence they actually possess is no better than the leaders they are blaming. Their inaction is a moral choice, not a procedural inevitability.
questions.quote: But the truth is, if your legislator won't support the abolition bill with the influence they have, they are no better than the committee chair, pro tem, and governor. Do not let them tell you otherwise.

questions.question: How do I answer the claim that there simply isn't enough time to hear an abolition bill?
questions.audience: Pastor mobilizing his congregation to contact representatives
questions.answer: Point to the frivolous bills that absorb legislative time. Gillespie cites a proposed cloth-napkin mandate for barbeque restaurants and a Bigfoot hunting season as examples of trivia the legislature has time to entertain. If they have time for that, they have time to consider laws protecting unborn children.
questions.quote: A few years ago, there was a bill filed to require that barbeque restaurants in Oklahoma use cloth napkins. Another bill would have implemented a Bigfoot hunting license and a Bigfoot hunting season. These are frivolous bills that are a waste of taxpayers' money and time.

questions.question: What does the inner culture of the Oklahoma Capitol look like, and why does it matter?
questions.audience: Citizen lobbyist visiting the Oklahoma Capitol for the first time
questions.answer: Gillespie compares the Capitol to a middle-school playground where chambers retaliate against one another and individual legislators refuse to vote for each other's bills out of personal grievance. Activists need to expect this immaturity rather than assume good-faith deliberation.
questions.quote: First, when you walk into the Capitol building you are walking onto the archetypal middle school playground. Almost every year, at some point the Senate gets mad at the House and refuses to hear any House bills until the House agrees to the Senate's demands…and vice versa.

questions.question: Is there proof that the Oklahoma Legislature can move quickly when leadership wants it to?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing whether to author or co-sponsor an abolition bill
questions.answer: Yes. When Governor Stitt prioritized Constitutional Carry in 2019, the legislature jettisoned its normal deadlines and put the bill on his desk in roughly a month. The same machinery is available for abolition if leaders treat it as a priority.
questions.quote: In 2019, when Governor Stitt took office, he let it be known that he wanted the bill allowing permitless carry of a handgun (Constitutional Carry) to be the first bill he signed into law. They threw out the process and deadlines and got that bill on the Governor's desk in about a month.

questions.question: What is the bottom-line test for whether the abolition of abortion is actually a legislator's priority?
questions.audience: Pastor mobilizing his congregation to contact representatives
questions.answer: Effort, not rhetoric. If the bill isn't moving, leadership has chosen not to move it. Procedural excuses are a tell that abolition is not on their priority list, and constituents should hold them accountable on that basis.
questions.quote: When it is a priority, they will make it happen. If it isn't happening, it isn't a priority. Do NOT let them tell you otherwise.

related.title: Why "No Bill But SB13" ?
related.reason: The companion strategic argument for concentrating abolitionist lobbying effort on a single Oklahoma abolition bill rather than diluting it across incremental measures.

related.title: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
related.reason: A real-world case study of the 'committee chair won't hear it' excuse Gillespie warns about, applied to Oklahoma's flagship abolition bill.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Illustrates the pro tem-level obstruction Gillespie cites as a common scapegoat, naming the Senate leader who exemplifies the pattern.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Identifies specific Oklahoma legislators using the very tactics this article teaches readers to refute.

related.title: Uncovered Letter Exposes Plan to Keep Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Documents the kind of behind-the-scenes collusion among legislators that Gillespie says often produces these procedural excuses.

related.title: "I am a pro-life Oklahoman who voted to table a bill that would abolish all abortion"
related.reason: A concrete instance of a legislator hiding behind procedure (tabling) to avoid abolishing abortion, the exact deflection Gillespie targets.

related.title: Does Romans 13 Really Instruct Christians To Allow the Killing of Babies?
related.reason: Tim Gillespie's other piece in the corpus, addressing the theological excuse that often accompanies the procedural ones cataloged here.
